name: Spring Boot Actuator Endpoints Exposed
description: Spring Boot Actuator exposes management endpoints (e.g., /actuator/env, /actuator/heapdump, /actuator/beans, /actuator/mappings) that can reveal environment variables, secrets, full class graphs, and live heap dumps when not properly secured. In many default configurations these endpoints are unauthenticated.
runtime_discovery: Crawl the running app for /actuator and its children (/actuator/env, /actuator/health, /actuator/heapdump, /actuator/mappings, /actuator/beans, /actuator/loggers, /actuator/trace, /actuator/httptrace). Enumerate both /actuator/* and legacy /management/* paths. Probe each without auth tokens.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 with JSON body containing keys like 'propertySources', 'contexts', 'beans', 'mappings', or a binary response (heapdump) from /actuator/heapdump. A 404 on /actuator but 200 on /actuator/health (partial exposure) is also flagged.

name: Actuator /env Leaks Credentials via Partial Masking Bypass
description: Even when /actuator/env masks secret values with '******', the property keys themselves (e.g., DB_PASSWORD, AWS_SECRET_KEY, STRIPE_SECRET) are still returned. Additionally, prior to Spring Boot 2.3, the /actuator/env/{name} single-property endpoint returns the raw unmasked value for some property sources.
runtime_discovery: GET /actuator/env without credentials. Then probe /actuator/env/{property-name} for individual entries (e.g., /actuator/env/spring.datasource.password). Compare key list against a dictionary of known secret property names.
detection_signal: Response body from /actuator/env contains property keys matching regex (password|secret|key|token|credential|api[-_]?key) even if values show '******'. A single-key probe /actuator/env/spring.datasource.password returning 200 with an unmasked value is a direct hit.

name: Broken Object-Level Authorization (IDOR) via Missing @PreAuthorize
description: Spring MVC REST endpoints that accept a resource ID in the path or query string (e.g., /api/orders/{id}) but rely only on URL-pattern security (HttpSecurity.antMatchers) without method-level @PreAuthorize checks allow any authenticated user to access any other user's resource by incrementing or fuzzing the ID.
runtime_discovery: Auth as user A, collect resource IDs from their normal responses. Auth as user B, substitute user A's IDs into identical requests. If user B receives user A's data, IDOR is confirmed. Also fuzz sequential/UUIDs in path and query parameters.
detection_signal: User B's request for /api/orders/{userA_id} returns HTTP 200 with user A's data rather than 403/404. Auth-differential comparison: same endpoint, same ID, different session tokens produces matching response bodies.

name: Mass Assignment via Spring Data / @RequestBody Binding
owasp_category: A08:2021 Software and Data Integrity Failures
description: When a Spring MVC controller uses @RequestBody to bind a JPA entity or DTO directly, an attacker can inject extra fields (e.g., 'role', 'isAdmin', 'accountBalance') into the JSON body that the application did not intend to be user-settable. Without explicit @JsonIgnoreProperties or a dedicated DTO, Spring's Jackson binding sets those fields.
runtime_discovery: Intercept a normal POST/PUT request body. Inject extra fields by appending 'role':'admin', 'isAdmin':true, 'enabled':true, 'balance':99999 to the JSON payload. Submit and observe whether the server reflects the injected values back in subsequent GET responses or login behavior changes.
detection_signal: A follow-up GET for the same resource returns the injected field value (e.g., 'role':'admin'). HTTP 200 on the mutating request with the injected field reflected in the response body. Elevation detected via auth-differential: re-auth after injection shows elevated permissions.

name: CSRF Protection Disabled in Spring Security Config
description: Spring Security enables CSRF protection by default, but many applications explicitly call http.csrf().disable() for REST API convenience. When the app also supports cookie-based sessions (as opposed to stateless JWT-only), this allows cross-site request forgery attacks against state-changing endpoints.
runtime_discovery: Authenticate via browser session. Check whether state-changing endpoints (POST /api/transfer, POST /api/settings) succeed without a CSRF token header or parameter. Observe Set-Cookie response header for session cookies lacking SameSite=Strict/Lax.
detection_signal: POST to a state-changing endpoint returns HTTP 200/201 when no X-XSRF-TOKEN or _csrf parameter is included, and the session cookie is present. Also: absence of XSRF-TOKEN cookie in Set-Cookie headers that would normally accompany Angular/Axios auto-CSRF.

name: H2 Database Console Exposed in Production
description: Spring Boot auto-configures the H2 in-memory database console at /h2-console when H2 is on the classpath and spring.h2.console.enabled=true. This web-based SQL console is often left enabled in staging/production, allowing unauthenticated full database access.
runtime_discovery: Probe /h2-console and /h2-console/login.jsp. Also check /console (alternate path). Attempt access without authentication.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 response from /h2-console with HTML containing 'H2 Console' title or a login form with 'JDBC URL' and 'User Name' fields. Any successful connection to the console without prior app authentication is a critical finding.

name: Open Redirect via Spring Security Login Redirect Parameter
description: Spring Security's default login flow accepts a redirect_to or spring-security-redirect parameter (or a custom 'continue' / 'next' query param) after authentication. If not validated against an allowlist of internal paths, an attacker can craft a login URL that redirects users to an external phishing site post-authentication.
runtime_discovery: Append ?redirect_to=https://evil.com, ?spring-security-redirect=https://evil.com, or ?continue=//evil.com to the login URL. Authenticate successfully and observe the Location header in the 302 response.
detection_signal: HTTP 302 Location header after successful login points to an external or non-origin URL (e.g., https://evil.com or //evil.com). Any redirect leaving the application's origin after auth is flagged.

name: JWT 'none' Algorithm Accepted by Spring Security
description: Some Spring Security JWT configurations (especially custom or older libraries) accept tokens with the 'none' algorithm or allow algorithm confusion (RS256 → HS256 downgrade), letting an attacker forge arbitrary JWT claims including elevated roles without knowing the signing secret.
runtime_discovery: Capture a valid JWT from a normal login response. Modify the header alg to 'none' and strip the signature. Also try alg:'HS256' with the public key as the HMAC secret. Replay the modified token on protected endpoints.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 on a protected endpoint when the tampered token (alg:none, no signature) is sent in the Authorization header. Any successful authenticated response with a forged/unsigned JWT is a critical hit.

name: JPQL / HQL Injection via Spring Data @Query
description: When Spring Data repositories use @Query annotations with string concatenation (instead of named parameters) or when controllers pass user input directly into EntityManager.createQuery(), attackers can inject JPQL/HQL fragments to bypass WHERE clauses, extract additional entity properties, or cause errors that reveal the schema.
runtime_discovery: Inject JPQL metacharacters into search/filter form fields and query parameters: single quote ('), ' OR '1'='1, ' AND 1=2--, LIKE %'. Observe whether the response changes (returns extra records), throws a 500 with a Hibernate exception, or changes record count.
detection_signal: HTTP 500 response body containing 'org.hibernate.', 'QuerySyntaxException', 'antlr', or 'unexpected token' when a single quote or JPQL keyword is injected. Alternatively, injecting ' OR '1'='1 causes the endpoint to return all records instead of filtered results.

name: Overly Permissive CORS Configuration with allowCredentials
description: Spring MVC @CrossOrigin or WebMvcConfigurer CORS configurations sometimes set allowedOrigins('*') combined with allowCredentials(true), or use regex origin reflection (echoing back any Origin header) which creates a nearly identical risk. This allows any site to make credentialed cross-origin requests.
runtime_discovery: Send requests with arbitrary Origin headers (Origin: https://evil.com) to API endpoints. Inspect the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response header — if it reflects back the attacker origin AND Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true is also present, the configuration is exploitable.
detection_signal: Response contains both 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.com' (reflected attacker origin) and 'Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true'. Any combination of a non-null, non-same-origin ACAO with ACAC:true is flagged.

name: Spring Security / Whitelabel Error Page Leaks Stack Traces
description: Spring Boot's default Whitelabel error page and unhandled exceptions surface full Java stack traces, exception messages, and internal class/package names in HTTP responses when server.error.include-stacktrace is not set to 'never'. This reveals framework versions, internal paths, and dependency information useful for chaining attacks.
runtime_discovery: Trigger errors by sending malformed JSON bodies, unexpected content types (Content-Type: text/xml instead of application/json), invalid path parameters (e.g., /api/users/notanumber for a Long param), and oversized payloads. Observe the response body for stack traces.
detection_signal: HTTP 500 response body containing 'java.lang.', 'org.springframework.', 'at com.', or a JSON object with 'trace' key containing multiline stack frame strings. Also: HTML response with 'Whitelabel Error Page' and an exception message.

name: Actuator /loggers Endpoint Allows Log Level Manipulation
description: The /actuator/loggers endpoint accepts POST requests to change log levels at runtime. An attacker with access to this endpoint can enable TRACE/DEBUG logging for security-sensitive packages (e.g., org.springframework.security) to cause the app to log passwords, tokens, and session IDs to logs they may also be able to read via /actuator/logfile.
runtime_discovery: GET /actuator/loggers to confirm access. Then POST to /actuator/loggers/org.springframework.security with body {"configuredLevel":"TRACE"}. Attempt to read /actuator/logfile afterward. Also attempt POST to /actuator/loggers/ROOT to set all logging to DEBUG.
detection_signal: HTTP 200 on POST to /actuator/loggers/{name} without authentication. Subsequent /actuator/logfile or /actuator/httptrace responses containing authentication credentials, tokens, or sensitive request parameters in log lines.

name: Vertical Privilege Escalation via Missing Role Checks on Admin Endpoints
description: Spring Security URL matchers use first-match semantics and are case-sensitive on some configurations. Admin endpoints like /api/admin/users might be protected by .antMatchers('/api/admin/**').hasRole('ADMIN') but bypassed via path variations (/api/Admin/users, /api/admin/users/, /api/admin/users;x=y) or HTTP verb confusion (POST vs GET).
runtime_discovery: With a low-privilege authenticated session, enumerate admin-looking paths discovered from error messages, JS source, or /actuator/mappings. Try path case variations, trailing slashes, semicolon injection, and double slashes. Compare HTTP response codes and body content between admin and non-admin sessions.
detection_signal: Low-privilege session receives HTTP 200 with admin-level data on a path variant (e.g., /API/Admin/users or /api/admin/users/) that the canonical path would have returned 403 for. Auth-differential: same path variant returns different body for admin vs regular user.

name: Session Fixation — Session ID Not Rotated After Authentication
description: When Spring Security's session fixation protection is misconfigured (SessionCreationPolicy.NEVER or sessionFixation().none()), the session ID issued before login is reused after successful authentication. An attacker who pre-plants a known session cookie can hijack the session once the victim logs in.
runtime_discovery: Record the session cookie (JSESSIONID) value from a pre-authentication request. Authenticate successfully. Compare the session cookie value in the post-authentication response — if it is identical to the pre-auth value, session fixation protection is absent.
detection_signal: JSESSIONID (or equivalent Spring Session cookie) value is identical before and after a successful POST to /login. The Set-Cookie header in the login success response is absent (no new cookie issued) or contains the same token value.

name: Spring Data REST Auto-Exports All Repositories as Unauthenticated HATEOAS Endpoints
description: Spring Data REST auto-exports every JPA repository as a discoverable REST endpoint under /api or a configured base path. Without explicit @RepositoryRestResource(exported=false) or method-level security, all entities including internal ones (users, roles, audit logs) are readable and writable by any caller.
runtime_discovery: GET the HATEOAS discovery root (/ or /api) to enumerate all auto-exported collection endpoints from _links. Probe each collection without authentication. Attempt GET (list), POST (create), PATCH (modify), DELETE on each endpoint as an unauthenticated user and as a low-privilege user.
detection_signal: Unauthenticated GET to /api returns JSON with _links listing multiple entity collections. Fetching any collection link without a session returns HTTP 200 with entity data. A POST or PATCH returning 201/200 without authentication or for a resource the user should not own is a direct finding.
